content.text: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) provides on-demand access to virtualized computing resources — virtual machines, managed disks, virtual networks, and load balancers — without requiring ownership of physical hardware. The customer controls the operating system and everything above it; the cloud provider manages the physical servers, hypervisors, and data center facilities.
IaaS is the lowest-level cloud service model. It abstracts physical hardware behind an API, giving users full control over the software stack from the OS upward. On Azure, IaaS primarily encompasses
(autoscaling VM pools). Together these services replicate the capabilities of a physical data center but provision in minutes through the Azure portal, CLI, or APIs.
A virtual machine in Azure is defined by its

— a combination of vCPU count, RAM, temporary disk size, and network bandwidth. The SKU determines the hourly cost. Managed Disks are durable block storage volumes that persist independently of the VM lifecycle; a disk can be detached from one VM and reattached to another. Virtual Networks provide private IP address space, subnets, routing, DNS, and the boundary within which resources communicate without traversing the public internet.

IaaS removes the hardware ownership barrier to running arbitrary software. Any application that runs on a Linux or Windows server can run on an Azure VM — legacy databases, specialized runtimes, commercial software with licensing requirements, custom kernel modules, or workloads that require specific CPU architectures (AVX-512 for ML inference, high memory ratios for SAP HANA). This makes IaaS the default migration path for on-premises applications that cannot easily be refactored for PaaS.

Beyond lift-and-shift, IaaS is the layer on which higher-level services are built. Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) orchestrates containers across a VM pool provisioned via IaaS. Database services run VMs internally but expose managed APIs. Understanding IaaS is therefore foundational for diagnosing performance issues, designing networks, and reasoning about costs even when using higher-level services.
— install any software, configure kernel parameters, use any runtime version; no restrictions imposed by a managed platform
— Azure offers hundreds of VM sizes spanning general-purpose, compute-optimized, memory-optimized, storage-optimized, GPU, and high-performance compute families
— upgrade to a larger SKU in minutes rather than waiting months for hardware procurement and installation
— teams with existing sysadmin skills can manage Azure VMs using the same tools (SSH, PowerShell, Ansible, Chef) they use on-premises
— VNets connect to on-premises networks via VPN Gateway or ExpressRoute, enabling gradual migration and split workloads
— Azure Spot VMs offer up to 90% discount for interruptible batch workloads; 1- or 3-year reservations save 30–60% on stable workloads
— OS patching, security hardening, log rotation, disk management, and runtime upgrades are entirely the customer's responsibility
— stopped (deallocated) VMs do not accrue compute charges, but Managed Disks and static IPs continue billing; an idle VM that is merely stopped (not deallocated) still charges full compute
— spinning up a new VM takes 1–3 minutes; fine for gradual load increases but insufficient for traffic spikes that need sub-second response
— VNets, subnets, NSGs, route tables, peering, private endpoints, and DNS require deliberate planning; misconfiguration leads to connectivity failures or security gaps
— a single VM has no SLA guarantee; high availability requires deploying across Availability Zones with a load balancer

When an Azure VM is created, the Azure Fabric Controller allocates physical capacity on a host server, provisions a virtual disk from a platform image (Windows Server, Ubuntu, RHEL, etc.) or a custom image, attaches a virtual network interface to the specified VNet/subnet, and boots the VM. The VM's OS sees virtual hardware: virtual CPUs backed by physical cores, virtual RAM, and virtual disks mapped to Managed Disk storage.
VM accrues full compute charges. A
VM releases its physical host allocation and stops charging for compute — only Managed Disks and public IP addresses continue billing. A
VM retains its host allocation and still accrues compute costs. Always use the
operation, not just OS shutdown, when wanting to pause costs.
Azure Managed Disks come in four performance tiers:
(low latency, high IOPS for production databases), and

(sub-millisecond latency, configurable IOPS/throughput for latency-sensitive workloads). Disk IOPS and throughput scale with disk size for Standard/Premium tiers. Ultra Disk allows setting IOPS and throughput independently of size.
A single Azure VM has no uptime SLA. To achieve the 99.99% SLA, VMs must be deployed across at least two
— physically separate data centers within the same region with independent power and cooling.

automate the deployment of multiple identical VMs across zones with autoscaling rules based on CPU percentage, queue depth, or custom metrics. VMSS supports rolling upgrades and automatic OS image updates, making it the standard pattern for stateless application tiers at scale.

The diagram below shows a three-tier IaaS web application architecture on Azure. Internet traffic enters through an Azure Load Balancer with a public IP, is distributed across a web-tier VM pool in a dedicated subnet, and forwarded to an application-tier VM pool in a separate subnet. Network Security Groups (NSGs) restrict which traffic is allowed to enter each subnet.

attrs.alt: Three-tier IaaS architecture on Azure showing Load Balancer, web-tier VMs, and app-tier VMs inside a Virtual Network

content.text: Each subnet carries an NSG acting as a stateful firewall. The web subnet allows inbound TCP 443 from any source; the app subnet allows inbound only from the web subnet's address range. A data-tier subnet (not shown) allows inbound only from the app subnet on the database port. This subnet isolation limits the blast radius of a compromised web VM — it cannot directly reach the database tier.
Choose IaaS when full control over the OS or runtime is required, or when an application cannot run on a managed PaaS platform. Specific scenarios:
— move on-premises VMs to Azure with minimal code change using Azure Migrate
— specific kernel versions, kernel modules, or commercial software (Oracle DB, SAP) that PaaS platforms cannot host
— ML training on NC/ND-series VMs with NVIDIA GPUs, or MPI clusters on HPC-series VMs with InfiniBand networking
— older applications with dependencies on specific OS versions or C runtime libraries that do not run on modern container runtimes

Do not choose IaaS when a PaaS alternative covers the use case. A web application that runs on a standard runtime (Node.js, Python, .NET, Java) is better served by Azure App Service: identical scaling, no OS management, and typically lower total cost of ownership. Similarly, prefer Azure Database for PostgreSQL over running PostgreSQL on a VM — the managed service handles backups, HA failover, and patching automatically.
Create a VM with Azure CLI
command provisions a VM with a new VNet, subnet, NSG, and public IP in a single operation. For production use, create these network resources explicitly beforehand to control their configuration.

# Create resource group
az group create --name vm-demo-rg --location eastus

# Create a virtual network and subnet
az network vnet create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --name myVNet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --subnet-name WebSubnet \
  --subnet-prefix 10.0.1.0/24

# Create a network security group and allow HTTPS
az network nsg create --resource-group vm-demo-rg --name WebNSG
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --nsg-name WebNSG \
  --name AllowHTTPS \
  --priority 100 \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --destination-port-range 443 \
  --access Allow

# Create the VM
az vm create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --name web-01 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D2s_v3 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --ssh-key-values ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet WebSubnet \
  --nsg WebNSG \
  --os-disk-size-gb 64 \
  --storage-sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1

# Get the public IP
az vm show --resource-group vm-demo-rg --name web-01 \
  --show-details --query publicIps --output tsv
For repeatable deployments, define infrastructure as Bicep templates. This example provisions a VM with a managed disk and associates it to an existing VNet subnet.

# Create a VM Scale Set across 3 availability zones
az vmss create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --name web-vmss \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --instance-count 2 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D2s_v3 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --ssh-key-values ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet WebSubnet \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --lb web-lb

# Configure autoscaling: scale out when CPU > 70%, scale in when CPU < 30%
az monitor autoscale create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --resource web-vmss \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets \
  --name web-autoscale \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 10 \
  --count 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --autoscale-name web-autoscale \
  --condition "Percentage CPU > 70 avg 5m" \
  --scale out 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --autoscale-name web-autoscale \
  --condition "Percentage CPU < 30 avg 10m" \
  --scale in 1
Managing VMs with the Azure Python SDK

import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.compute.models import VirtualMachineInstanceView

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
subscription_id = os.environ["AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"]
compute_client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

RG = "vm-demo-rg"

# List all VMs and their power states
for vm in compute_client.virtual_machines.list(RG):
    iv: VirtualMachineInstanceView = compute_client.virtual_machines.instance_view(
        RG, vm.name
    )
    statuses = {s.code: s.display_status for s in iv.statuses}
    power = statuses.get("PowerState/running", statuses.get("PowerState/deallocated", "unknown"))
    print(f"{vm.name:20s}  {vm.hardware_profile.vm_size:25s}  {power}")

# Deallocate a VM to stop billing (not just OS-shutdown)
poller = compute_client.virtual_machines.begin_deallocate(RG, "web-01")
poller.result()
print("VM deallocated — compute charges stopped")

# Resize a VM to a larger SKU
compute_client.virtual_machines.begin_update(
    RG, "web-01",
    {"hardware_profile": {"vm_size": "Standard_D4s_v3"}},
).result()
print("VM resized to Standard_D4s_v3")
Attach and expand a Managed Disk

# Create a 512 GB Premium SSD data disk
az disk create \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --name data-disk-01 \
  --size-gb 512 \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1

# Attach to a running VM
az vm disk attach \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --vm-name web-01 \
  --name data-disk-01

# SSH into VM and format + mount the disk
# Find the new disk device (usually /dev/sdc for first attached data disk)
# sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdc
# sudo mkdir /data && sudo mount /dev/sdc /data
# Add to /etc/fstab for persistence across reboots

# Later: expand the disk (VM must be stopped/deallocated for OS disk expansion)
az disk update \
  --resource-group vm-demo-rg \
  --name data-disk-01 \
  --size-gb 1024
# Then resize the partition inside the OS: sudo resize2fs /dev/sdc
A financial services company migrates an Oracle Database workload to Azure. Oracle is not available as a managed PaaS service on Azure, so IaaS is the only viable path. The team provisions an

VM (128 vCPUs, 2 TiB RAM) for the database server, with multiple Ultra Disks configured for sub-millisecond I/O latency matching on-premises storage performance. A second standby VM in a different AZ runs Oracle Data Guard for automatic failover. The web application tier runs on a VM Scale Set of

VMs behind an Application Gateway with WAF. Azure Site Recovery replicates the entire VM configuration to a paired region for disaster recovery. Total migration timeline: 12 weeks, achieving the same performance as on-premises at 35% lower annual infrastructure cost due to elimination of hardware refresh and data center power contracts.
— using the OS shutdown command or the portal's "Stop" button does not deallocate the VM; compute billing continues. Always use
— a lone VM gets no uptime SLA; planned maintenance events will cause downtime. Always deploy production workloads across at least two AZs behind a load balancer
— development environments frequently use production VM sizes and run 24/7, accumulating substantial bills; use the smallest adequate SKU and schedule auto-shutdown during off-hours
— database VMs should have no public IP and accept connections only from application-tier subnets via NSG rules; never expose database ports to the internet
— unlike PaaS, IaaS VMs require customer-managed patching; enable Azure Update Manager or configure automatic OS image upgrades on VMSS to prevent security vulnerabilities
Using local (temporary) disk for persistent data
— Azure VMs include a temporary disk (
on Linux) that is ephemeral and wiped on VM reallocation; use Managed Disks for any data that must survive a restart or VM stop/start cycle
Use managed identities instead of stored credentials for VMs that need to access Azure services (Key Vault, Storage, databases); eliminate service principal secrets from VM environments entirely
Enable Azure Bastion for SSH/RDP access instead of exposing port 22/3389 on public IPs; Bastion provides browser-based terminal access through the Azure portal over TLS
Deploy VMSS with zone-balancing enabled for production web tiers; configure a minimum of 2 instances to maintain availability during scale-in events
Choose Premium SSD for OS disks of production VMs; Standard HDD increases VM boot time, risks IOPS throttling under load, and provides no redundancy guarantee
Automate patching with Azure Update Manager; schedule maintenance windows during off-peak hours and test updates in a staging VMSS before rolling out to production
Use custom VM images (golden images) baked with Packer to standardize OS configuration, security baselines, and installed software across all VMs in a scale set
VM performance is bounded by three resources: compute (vCPU/RAM), network bandwidth (Mbps), and disk IOPS/throughput. Each VM SKU publishes documented limits for all three. For example, a

provides 4 vCPUs, 16 GiB RAM, 6,400 cached IOPS, and 3,200 uncached IOPS. These limits are hard ceilings; a workload that exceeds disk IOPS limits will be throttled, causing elevated latency. Matching the VM SKU to the workload's actual resource profile — not just compute — is essential for predictable performance.

Accelerated Networking (available on most general-purpose SKUs) bypasses the hypervisor's virtual switch and delivers the network interface directly to the VM using SR-IOV, reducing latency from ~500µs to ~50µs and increasing maximum throughput. It should be enabled on all production VMs. For highest-throughput storage, co-locating VMs and managed disks in the same AZ eliminates cross-AZ storage latency. Proximity Placement Groups pin VMs to the same physical cluster for lowest possible inter-VM latency in MPI or microservice architectures.
— discovers, assesses, and migrates on-premises VMware/Hyper-V/physical VMs to Azure with automated replication
— centralized OS patch management across all VMs in a subscription with compliance reporting and scheduled maintenance windows
— managed jump server providing SSH and RDP access to VMs without requiring a public IP on the target VM
— replicates VM disks to a secondary region and enables automated failover for disaster recovery with RPO of seconds
— HashiCorp's image builder for creating Azure Marketplace-compatible custom VM images with pre-installed software and hardened OS configuration
— configuration management tools that work with Azure VMs via SSH the same way as physical servers
IaaS places the full security responsibility for the OS and software stack on the customer. The provider secures the physical host and hypervisor. Key security controls for Azure VMs:
— stateful L4 firewall rules attached to subnets or NICs; define explicit allow rules and deny all other traffic. NSGs are the primary perimeter around VM workloads

— installs an agent on VMs and provides threat detection, vulnerability assessment, file integrity monitoring, and just-in-time VM access (limits port 22/3389 exposure to specific IPs for a time window)
— encrypts OS and data disks using BitLocker (Windows) or DM-Crypt (Linux) with keys stored in Azure Key Vault; enabled via a single CLI command and required for most compliance frameworks
— distributing a Scale Set across 3 AZs maintains capacity even if one zone experiences a complete outage; the zonal SLA rises to 99.99% with 2+ zones

— application-consistent VM backups stored in a Recovery Services vault; supports backup policies (daily, weekly, monthly retention), cross-region restore, and soft-delete protection against accidental deletion

Think of IaaS as renting an empty apartment versus renting a furnished flat (PaaS) or staying in a hotel (SaaS). The empty apartment (IaaS) gives full control over furniture, layout, and decor — but you furnish, clean, and repair everything yourself. The furnished flat (PaaS) arrives with appliances, furniture, and a maintenance team that handles repairs, but you cannot rearrange the kitchen layout. The hotel (SaaS) handles everything but you cannot customize anything. IaaS is the right choice when your application is the furniture — when you need to bring custom software that won't fit inside a pre-furnished platform.

IaaS gives full OS control and is the migration path for workloads that cannot run on managed PaaS platforms — legacy software, custom runtimes, GPU workloads, and applications with specific kernel requirements
Always deallocate VMs when not in use — stopped (non-deallocated) VMs continue accruing compute charges at the full hourly rate
A single VM has no SLA; production workloads require deployment across two or more Availability Zones behind a load balancer to achieve the 99.99% uptime guarantee
VM Scale Sets with autoscaling rules automate horizontal scaling; configure both scale-out (CPU > threshold) and scale-in (CPU < threshold) rules with cooling periods to prevent thrashing
OS patching, disk management, security hardening, and runtime upgrades are entirely the customer's responsibility — enable Azure Update Manager and Microsoft Defender for Servers from day one
Prefer PaaS over IaaS whenever possible — App Service, AKS, and managed databases eliminate the operational overhead of VM management while providing equivalent or better scalability and availability
